title: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Global Epidemiology and Leading Causes of HCC
items.content: Liver cancer is a significant global health issue, ranking as the sixth most common cancer worldwide. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) constitutes 85% of primary liver cancers, with high incidence rates in Asia and sub-Saharan Africa due to hepatitis B virus (HBV) prevalence.
keyPoints: Liver cancer is the third leading cause of cancer-related deaths globally.
Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) represents ~85% of primary liver cancers.
Highest HCC incidence rates are in Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, with 20–35 cases per 100,000 inhabitants.
In the United States, there are approximately 40,000 new liver cancer cases annually.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Risk Factors for HCC Development
items.content: The main risk factors for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) include cirrhosis, chronic HBV or HCV infection, alcohol abuse, metabolic syndrome, and hemochromatosis. Cirrhotic patients have a 3–8% annual risk of developing HCC if infected with HBV or HCV.
keyPoints: Cirrhosis is a primary risk factor for HCC development.
HBV infection accounts for ~50% of global HCC cases, particularly in Asia and Africa.
HCV infection is responsible for ~30% of cases, mainly in Europe and North America.
Annual risk of HCC in HBV- or HCV-infected cirrhotic patients is 3–8%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.title: Emerging Risk Factors: Metabolic Syndrome and MASH
items.content: Metabolic syndrome, including diabetes and obesity, contributes to ~30% of HCC cases. Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is a rapidly growing cause of cirrhosis in developed countries, accounting for 15–20% of Western HCC cases.
keyPoints: Metabolic syndrome accounts for ~30% of HCC cases.
MASH-related cirrhosis represents ~15–20% of HCC cases in the West.
Annual incidence of HCC in MASH-related cirrhosis is 1–2%/year.
25–30% of MASH-associated HCCs occur without cirrhosis.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.title: Key Molecular Drivers in HCC Pathogenesis
items.content: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) development involves a multistep process from low-grade dysplastic nodules to high-grade dysplastic nodules and eventually early-stage HCC. The key molecular drivers identified include mutations in the TERT promoter, TP53, and CTNNB1, which are pivotal but currently unactionable with existing therapies.
keyPoints: HCC results from 40–60 somatic genomic alterations per tumor, with 4–8 being driver cancer genes.
Main molecular drivers: TERT promoter (56%), TP53 (27%), CTNNB1 (26%).
EGFR, HER2, PIK3CA, BRAF, KRAS mutations are rare (<5%) in HCC.
~20-25% of HCCs have at least one actionable mutation.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Molecular Subclasses and Immune Classes in HCC
items.content: HCC can be classified into two molecular subclasses: proliferative, associated with poor outcomes and HBV-related etiologies, and nonproliferative, characterized by CTNNB1 mutations and better outcomes. Additionally, an immune classification distinguishes inflamed (hot) tumors from noninflamed (cold) tumors based on immune infiltration and response to immunotherapies.
keyPoints: Two molecular subclasses: proliferative (~50%) and nonproliferative (~50%).
Proliferative subclass linked with poor outcomes, HBV, and overexpression of $\alpha$-fetoprotein.
Nonproliferative subclass characterized by CTNNB1 mutations and better outcomes.
Inflamed HCC class (~35%): immune infiltrate, PD-1/PD-L1 expression, better response to immunotherapies.
Noninflamed (cold) tumors: associated with immune escape pathways or lack of T-cell infiltration.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Primary Prevention of Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC)
items.content: Prevention of HCC can be significantly achieved through vaccination and effective treatment of HBV and HCV infections. Universal HBV vaccination, recommended for all newborns and high-risk groups, leads to a notable decrease in HCC incidence.
keyPoints: Universal HBV vaccination is associated with a significant decrease in HCC incidence.
Effective antiviral treatments for chronic HBV result in 50–80% risk reduction of HCC development.
Treatment of HCV with DAAs yields >90% SVR rates, reducing HCC occurrence.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.title: Surveillance Strategies for Early Detection of HCC
items.content: Surveillance programs aim to reduce cancer-related mortality by enabling early detection, which enhances the effectiveness and cost-efficiency of curative therapies. Recommended surveillance includes ultrasonography every 6 months with serum AFP levels.
keyPoints: Ultrasonography every 6 months with serum AFP is recommended for HCC surveillance.
Sensitivity of ~65% and specificity >90% for early detection using this method.
Surveillance is cost-effective but engages only ~50% in Europe and ~30% in the U.S.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis Timing of HCC
items.content: HCC is generally diagnosed at early or intermediate stages in Western countries due to surveillance, whereas it is often detected at advanced stages in most Asian and African countries.
keyPoints: Surveillance programs detect early HCC in 70–80% of cases.
Without surveillance, HCC is usually discovered at an advanced stage with a median life expectancy of <1 year.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.title: Noninvasive Diagnosis Criteria for Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) in Cirrhotic Patients
items.content: In cirrhotic patients, noninvasive diagnosis of HCC is based on imaging techniques such as four-phase multidetector CT or dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI. These methods identify the typical vascular uptake pattern of HCC nodules: arterial phase uptake with washout in portal venous or delayed phases, which has a diagnostic specificity of ~95-100%. This high specificity often makes biopsy unnecessary.
keyPoints: Noninvasive diagnosis applies only to cirrhotic patients using four-phase multidetector CT or dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI.
Typical HCC radiological pattern: arterial phase uptake with washout in portal venous/delayed phases.
Diagnostic specificity of this pattern is ~95-100%, often negating the need for biopsy.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.title: LI-RADS Classification and Management Recommendations
items.content: The Liver Imaging Reporting and Data System (LI-RADS) classifies liver nodules based on their probability of being HCC. Nodules >10 mm are categorized, with LI-RADS-1 indicating a 0% probability of HCC, LI-RADS-4 suggesting a 60-70% likelihood, and LI-RADS-5 indicating ~95% probability. For LI-RADS-4 lesions, repeated imaging or biopsy is recommended within 3-6 months.
keyPoints: LI-RADS classifies nodules >10 mm based on HCC probability: LI-RADS-1 (0%), LI-RADS-4 (60-70%), LI-RADS-5 (~95%).
For LI-RADS-4 lesions, repeat imaging or biopsy within 3-6 months is recommended.
LI-RADS-M category includes malignant features but only ~35% are HCC.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.title: Improved Survival Outcomes in HCC with New Therapies
items.content: Recent advancements in the treatment of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) have significantly increased survival rates. For early-stage HCC, options such as resection, liver transplantation, and local ablation can extend median overall survival beyond 5 years. Additionally, adjuvant therapy with atezolizumab plus bevacizumab has been shown to improve recurrence-free survival in high-risk patients undergoing resection or ablation.
keyPoints: Resection, liver transplantation, and local ablation can extend median OS beyond 5 years for early-stage HCC.
Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab improves recurrence-free survival post-resection/ablation in high-risk patients.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Enhanced Progression-Free Survival with Combined Therapies for Intermediate HCC
items.content: For intermediate-stage HCC, transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) has improved the natural history from approximately 16 months to around 30 months. When combined with durvalumab plus bevacizumab, TACE further enhances progression-free survival.
keyPoints: TACE improves median survival for intermediate-stage HCC from ~16 months to ~30 months.
Combination of TACE with durvalumab plus bevacizumab increases progression-free survival.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Systemic Therapies Extend Survival in Advanced HCC
items.content: In advanced-stage HCC, systemic therapies such as atezolizumab plus bevacizumab, durvalumab plus tremelimumab, sorafenib, lenvatinib, regorafenib, cabozantinib, and ramucirumab have significantly improved median survival. Front-line treatments can extend survival beyond 19 months, while second-line therapies offer a median of 10 months.
keyPoints: Systemic drugs improve median survival in advanced HCC from 8 months to >19 months (front-line) and 10 months (second-line).
Key systemic therapies include atezolizumab plus bevacizumab, durvalumab plus tremelimumab, sorafenib, lenvatinib, regorafenib, cabozantinib, and ramucirumab.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Curative Therapies for Early-Stage HCC
items.content: Patients diagnosed at stages 0 and A of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) are eligible for potentially curative therapies such as resection, transplantation, or local ablation. These treatments significantly improve median survival rates to 60 months or more compared to historical controls.
keyPoints: 40% of patients diagnosed at stages 0 and A
Median survival rates of 60 months or beyond with curative therapies
Historical control outcomes: 36 months
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.title: Systemic Treatments for Advanced HCC
items.content: Patients at advanced stage C of HCC benefit from systemic treatments. First-line therapy includes combinations like atezolizumab plus bevacizumab or durvalumab plus tremelimumab, which have shown superiority over sorafenib in phase 3 trials.
keyPoints: First-line therapy: atezolizumab plus bevacizumab or durvalumab plus tremelimumab
Superior to sorafenib in phase 3 trials
Alternative options for contraindications: sorafenib or lenvatinib
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.title: Resection in Noncirrhotic and Cirrhotic Patients with Early-Stage HCC
items.content: Surgical resection is the first-line treatment for noncirrhotic patients with early-stage hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) classified as BCLC 0 or A, particularly when they have solitary tumors. In cirrhotic patients with BCLC 0 tumors smaller than 2 cm in diameter, local ablation using radiofrequency (RF) may be considered due to cost-effectiveness benefits. For single HCC tumors larger than 2 cm (BCLC A), resection remains the primary treatment option for Child-Pugh A patients who have normal bilirubin levels and no portal hypertension.
keyPoints: First-line treatment: Resection for noncirrhotic patients with early-stage HCC (BCLC 0 or A) with solitary tumors.
Ablation vs. resection: For BCLC 0 tumors <2 cm in cirrhotic patients, local ablation competes with resection; cost-effectiveness favors RF ablation.
Resection criteria for single tumors >2 cm (BCLC A): Child-Pugh A status, normal bilirubin, no portal hypertension (no esophageal/gastric varices or platelet count >100,000/μL with splenomegaly).
Perioperative outcomes: Decompensation rate of 5%, mortality <1%, and 5-year survival of 60–70%.
Contraindications for resection: Macrovascular invasion, extrahepatic involvement, liver dysfunction (Child-Pugh B-C).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.title: Adjuvant Therapy Post-Resection or Ablation in HCC
items.content: Tumor recurrence is a significant issue following resection and local ablation, with 5-year recurrence rates ranging from 50–70%. Predictors of recurrence include tumor size, number, presence of microsatellites, or microvascular invasion. Adjuvant therapy with atezolizumab plus bevacizumab for 12 months has been shown to improve recurrence-free survival in high-risk patients and is now part of clinical guidelines.
keyPoints: Recurrence rates post-resection/ablation: 50–70% at 5 years.
Predictors of recurrence: Tumor size, number, microsatellites, microvascular invasion.
Adjuvant therapy benefit: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab for 12 months improves recurrence-free survival in high-risk patients.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.title: Liver Transplantation Criteria and Limitations in HCC Treatment
items.content: Liver transplantation is the preferred treatment for cirrhotic patients with single tumors ≤5 cm or small multinodular tumors (three or fewer nodules, each ≤3 cm), known as Milan criteria. These criteria are associated with median survival times of 10 years and a perioperative mortality rate of <3%. However, limitations include cost, donor availability, and geographic variability in deceased liver donation rates. Neoadjuvant locoregional therapies may be used to downstage tumors when waiting times exceed 6 months.
keyPoints: Preferred treatment: Liver transplantation for cirrhotic patients with single tumors ≤5 cm or small multinodular tumors (≤3 nodules, each ≤3 cm).
Milan criteria outcomes: Median survival of 10 years, perioperative mortality <3%.
Limitations: Cost, donor availability, geographic variability in deceased liver donation rates.
Neoadjuvant therapy recommendation: Locoregional therapies for waiting times >6 months to downstage tumors.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.title: Survival and Efficacy of RF Ablation for HCC
items.content: Radiofrequency (RF) ablation is a locoregional therapy used to treat hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), particularly effective in tumors <2 cm. It induces coagulative necrosis by heating tissue, creating a safety ring that may eliminate small undetected satellites.
keyPoints: 5-year survival rates for HCC patients treated with RF ablation are ~60%.
Complete responses achieved in 90–100% of cases for tumors <2 cm using RF or microwave ablation (MWA).
Competes with resection in terms of cost-effectiveness as a first-line option.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.title: TACE as First-Line Treatment for Intermediate Stage HCC
items.content: Transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) is the primary treatment for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma at intermediate stages, specifically BCLC stage B. It involves local hepatic artery administration of chemotherapy followed by arterial obstruction.
keyPoints: Median survival in selected populations ranges from 20-30 months with TACE compared to 16 months for control arms.
Objective response rates are between 50-70%.
TACE is associated with a postembolization syndrome affecting ~50% of patients and major complications in <5%, with treatment-related death in <2%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.title: Advantages of TARE Over TACE for Unresectable HCC
items.content: Transarterial radioembolization (TARE) with yttrium-90 is an alternative to TACE, especially suitable for patients with portal vein thrombosis. It uses beads coated with Y-90 that emit short-range beta radiation.
keyPoints: TARE has a safety profile similar to TACE and can be used in patients with portal vein thrombosis.
Around 20% of patients experience liver-related toxicity, with <2% experiencing treatment-related death.
Encouraging survival results have been reported in phase II studies.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.title: First-Line Therapy for Advanced HCC: Atezolizumab Plus Bevacizumab
items.content: Atezolizumab combined with bevacizumab has become the standard first-line therapy for advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), showing improved survival, progression-free survival, and quality of life compared to sorafenib.
keyPoints: Median overall survival (OS) with atezolizumab plus bevacizumab is 19.2 months versus 13.4 months for sorafenib.
Objective response rate is higher at 30% compared to 12% for sorafenib.
Grade 3-4 adverse events are less frequent (36%) than with sorafenib (50%).
Common side effects include hypertension, proteinuria, and low-grade diarrhea; autoimmune events are infrequent.
15% of patients discontinue treatment due to adverse events.
Screening for varices is recommended before initiating therapy to mitigate bleeding risks.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.title: Alternative First-Line Therapy: Tremelimumab Plus Durvalumab
items.content: The combination of tremelimumab and durvalumab offers a survival advantage over sorafenib in advanced HCC, even for patients with portal hypertension or varices.
keyPoints: Median OS is 16.4 months compared to 13.7 months for sorafenib.
Response rate is significantly higher at 20.1% versus 5.1% with sorafenib.
More immune-related adverse events occur, requiring glucocorticoid treatment in 20% of patients.
Can be administered to patients with portal hypertension or varices.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.title: Second-Line Therapy Options for HCC Post-Sorafenib
items.content: For patients progressing on sorafenib, regorafenib, cabozantinib, and ramucirumab have shown survival benefits over placebo.
keyPoints: Only half of patients progressing on sorafenib are eligible for second-line therapies.
Median survival without treatment is 7-8 months.
FDA-approved additional treatments include pembrolizumab and nivolumab plus ipilimumab based on phase 2 data.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.title: Regorafenib's Survival Benefit in Advanced HCC Post-Sorafenib
items.content: Regorafenib extends survival for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) who have progressed on sorafenib, increasing median survival from 7.8 to 10.6 months.
keyPoints: Survival increase from 7.8 to 10.6 months
HR of 0.62 indicating a 38% reduction in risk of death
Response rate: 10%
Median time on treatment: 3.5 months
Higher prevalence of toxicity compared to sorafenib, but only 10% discontinuation due to adverse events
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.title: Cabozantinib's Role in HCC Post-Sorafenib Progression
items.content: Cabozantinib improves survival for patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma who have progressed on sorafenib, extending median survival to 10.2 months compared to 8.0 months with placebo.
keyPoints: Survival improvement from 8.0 to 10.2 months
HR of 0.76 indicating a reduction in risk of death
Manageable toxicity profile, common grade 3-4 events include palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia and hypertension
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA)
items.content: Cholangiocarcinoma (CCA) is a significant liver cancer with varying incidence rates globally. It ranks as the second most common liver cancer after hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), with a notably low 5-year survival rate of 10%. The incidence varies widely, from 1–2 cases per 100,000 in Europe and North America to over 80 cases/100,000 in parts of Southeast Asia. Risk factors include primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) and parasitic infections like Opisthorchis viverrini, with antihelminthic therapy such as praziquantel being a preventive measure.
keyPoints: CCA is the second most common liver cancer after HCC.
5-year survival rate for CCA is 10%.
Incidence ranges from 1–2 cases per 100,000 in Europe/North America to >80 cases/100,000 in Southeast Asia.
Male-to-female ratio of CCA is 1.2:1.
Only 30% of CCA patients have a known risk factor.
Risk factors include PSC, biliary duct cysts, hepatolithiasis, Caroli's disease, and parasitic infestation with flukes.
Praziquantel can prevent fluke-related CCA in Asia.
PSC has a lifetime incidence of iCCA development ranging from 5 to 10%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.title: Molecular Subclassification and Targetable Mutations in Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA)
items.content: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) can be subclassified based on molecular traits into large duct and small duct types, each associated with distinct mutations. The large duct type often has IDH (15–20%) and FGFR2 (15%) mutations, correlating with a better prognosis. Conversely, the small duct type is linked to KRAS (15%) and SMARD4 (<5%) mutations, indicating a poorer prognosis. Up to 40% of iCCAs have targetable mutations, which opens avenues for personalized treatment strategies.
keyPoints: iCCA can be subclassified into large duct and small duct types based on molecular traits.
Large duct type is associated with IDH (15–20%) and FGFR2 (15%) mutations.
Small duct type is linked to KRAS (15%) and SMARD4 (<5%) mutations.
Up to 40% of iCCAs have targetable mutations.
P53 (~30%) and KRAS (~25%) are common mutations in pCCA/dCCA.
ERBB2 amplifications (~20%) are common in gallbladder cancer.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.title: Surveillance Recommendations for At-Risk Populations
items.content: For patients with primary biliary sclerosis (PBS) and those with cirrhosis or liver fluke infection, surveillance is recommended to facilitate early diagnosis of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA). Surveillance involves specific imaging and biomarker testing at defined intervals.
keyPoints: Primary biliary sclerosis patients should undergo CA 19-9 measurement and MRCP every 12 months.
Patients with cirrhosis or liver fluke infection should have abdominal ultrasound every 6 months.
Surveillance aims to detect iCCA early in at-risk populations.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.title: Diagnostic and Staging Approach for Confirmed iCCA
items.content: Once pathological confirmation of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) is achieved, specific imaging modalities are recommended to assess disease extent. Immunohistochemistry aids in distinguishing iCCA from other malignancies.
keyPoints: Imaging includes abdominal MRI, chest and abdomen CT scan, and PET scan for staging.
PET scanning helps identify lymph node metastasis when not apparent on MRI/CT.
Immunohistochemical markers: K7, K19, K20 confirm iCCA; Hep-Par-1, GPC3, HSP70 suggest mixed HCC-iCCA tumors.
CA 19-9 cutoff level of 100 U/mL has prognostic significance but lacks early diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity and specificity ~60%).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.title: Adjuvant Therapy with Capecitabine for Resectable CCA
items.content: For patients who have undergone resection of cholangiocarcinoma (CCA), adjuvant therapy with capecitabine is recommended to improve survival outcomes. This recommendation stems from the BILCAP trial, which demonstrated a significant improvement in median survival when capecitabine was used as an adjuvant treatment.
keyPoints: Adjuvant capecitabine for 6 months improves survival (53 vs 36 months; adjusted HR, 0.75).
American Society of Clinical Oncology guidelines recommend this therapy post-resection.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.title: Triplet Therapy as Standard for Advanced CCA
items.content: In advanced stages of cholangiocarcinoma, particularly in patients with ECOG performance status 0–1, triplet therapy combining gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab is considered the standard treatment. This regimen has shown significantly better survival and progression-free survival compared to dual chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Triplet therapy improves median survival (12.8 vs 11.5 months) and progression-free survival over gemcitabine plus cisplatin alone.
Objective response rate with triplet therapy is 27%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.title: Second-Line Therapy Options for iCCA
items.content: For patients progressing after initial treatment, second-line therapies include mFOLFOX and molecular/immune therapies targeting specific genetic mutations. Pembrolizumab is approved for MSI-H or dMMR solid tumors that have progressed following prior treatments.
keyPoints: mFOLFOX improves median OS from 5.3 to 6.2 months (adjusted HR, 0.69).
Pembrolizumab shows improved median OS in MSI-H/dMMR tumors (12.7 vs 10.9 months; HR, 0.83).
Approved therapies for iCCA with IDH1/2 mutations, FGFR2 aberrations, or dMMR/MSI-H.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.title: Diagnosis and Imaging Approach for Cholangiocarcinoma
items.content: The diagnostic approach for perihilar (pCCA) and distal cholangiocarcinomas (dCCAs) involves a combination of clinical presentation, imaging studies, and pathology. Acute onset of painless jaundice is common in pCCA, while dCCA can mimic early pancreatic cancer. Imaging with CT and MRI has high sensitivity (>85%) for assessing bile duct involvement and vascular invasion. MRI cholangiography provides detailed information on the extent of bile duct lesions.
keyPoints: Acute onset of painless jaundice occurs in 90% of pCCA patients.
CA 19-9 levels >129 U/mL are suspicious for CCA.
CT and MRI have sensitivity and specificity >85% for bile duct involvement.
MRI cholangiography is optimal for defining bile duct lesion extent.
FISH enhances cytology sensitivity from 20% to ~40%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.title: Treatment Strategies and Prognosis for Cholangiocarcinoma
items.content: The treatment of cholangiocarcinoma depends on the tumor's location, stage, and lymph node involvement. For pCCA with negative lymph nodes, surgery or transplantation is curative. Staging laparoscopy is recommended to exclude metastatic disease before surgery. Resection involves hepatic and bile duct removal with Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. Liver transplantation may be considered for unresectable single pCCAs <3 cm without dissemination, offering ~70% 5-year survival. For dCCA, surgical resection (Whipple procedure) is primary, with median survival of 24 months and ~25% 5-year survival. Adjuvant therapy with capecitabine is based on the BILCAP study.
keyPoints: Surgery or transplantation for pCCA with negative lymph nodes.
Staging laparoscopy recommended to exclude metastatic disease before surgery.
Resection involves hepatic and bile duct removal with Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy.
Liver transplantation considered for unresectable single pCCAs <3 cm, ~70% 5-year survival.
Whipple procedure for dCCA offers median survival of 24 months and ~25% 5-year survival.
Adjuvant therapy with capecitabine based on BILCAP study.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.title: Risk Factors and Incidence of Gallbladder Cancer
items.content: Cholelithiasis is the major risk factor for gallbladder cancer, with less than 1% of patients developing this malignancy. The estimated number of cases in the U.S. in 2016 was 11,400, surpassing cholangiocarcinoma (CCA). Gallbladder polyps larger than 10 mm are at higher risk of malignant transformation.
keyPoints: Cholelithiasis is a major risk factor for gallbladder cancer.
<1% of patients with cholelithiasis develop gallbladder cancer.
Estimated U.S. cases in 2016: 11,400.
Gallbladder polyps ≥10 mm at higher risk of malignancy.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.title: Staging and Treatment Options for Gallbladder Cancer
items.content: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography is the most accurate technique to define staging of gallbladder cancer. Surgical resection, either simple or radical cholecystectomy, is the mainstay treatment for local disease, with ~20% of patients eligible for curative surgery. Adjuvant therapy with capecitabine is recommended in R0 cases.
keyPoints: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography defines staging accurately.
~20% of patients are candidates for surgical resection with curative intent.
5-year survival rates range from 60 to 90%, depending on prognostic factors.
Adjuvant therapy with capecitabine is recommended in R0 cases.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Fibrolamellar Hepatocellular Carcinoma
items.content: Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma (FLC) is a rare liver cancer affecting young adults without underlying liver disease. It accounts for 0.85% of primary hepatic malignancies in the U.S., with an incidence rate of 0.02 cases per 100,000 inhabitants. Surgical resection is the mainstay treatment, and FLC has a better prognosis than typical hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
keyPoints: FLC affects children and young adults (10–30 years) without background liver disease.
Accounts for 0.85% of primary hepatic malignancies in the U.S.
Incidence rate: 0.02 cases per 100,000 inhabitants.
Median survival after resection is 70 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.title: Management of Hemangiomas in Different Patient Populations
items.content: Hemangiomas are common benign liver tumors, present in ~5% of the general population. They are typically diagnosed by ultrasound but require contrast-enhanced imaging (contrast-enhanced ultrasound, CT, or MRI) in cirrhotic and oncology patients due to diagnostic challenges.
keyPoints: Hemangiomas prevalence: ~5% of the general population
Diagnosis method: Ultrasound for most; contrast-enhanced imaging required for cirrhotic/oncology patients
Management: Conservative, follow-up not recommended unless symptomatic due to compression
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hepatocellular Adenomas (HCA)
items.content: HCAs are rare benign liver tumors with a prevalence of 0.001%. They predominantly affect women aged 35–40 years, often associated with oral contraceptive use or anabolic steroid use in men. HCAs can lead to hemorrhage and HCC development if >5 cm.
keyPoints: HCA prevalence: 0.001% of the population
Risk factors: Oral contraceptives (females), anabolic steroids (males)
Diagnosis: MRI correlates with molecular subtypes in 80% of cases
Treatment: Discontinue oral contraceptives, weight loss recommended; resection for >5 cm or CTNNB1 mutation
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: Which region has the highest incidence rates of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) due to hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection?
items.explanation: The highest incidence rates of HCC occur in Asia and sub-Saharan Africa due to the high prevalence of HBV infection.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: What percentage of primary liver cancers does hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) represent?
items.explanation: HCC represents 85% of primary liver cancers.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: Which virus is responsible for approximately 30% of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) cases globally?
items.explanation: HCV infection is responsible for ~30% of HCC cases globally.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.question: Which factor is associated with a strong male preponderance in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) incidence?
items.explanation: HCC has a strong male preponderance, with a male-to-female ratio estimated to be 2.5:1.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which gene mutation is most frequently associated with aflatoxin B1 exposure in HCC patients?
items.explanation: TP53 mutations, specifically the R249S hotspot mutation, are most frequently associated with aflatoxin B1 exposure.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What percentage of HCCs have at least one actionable mutation?
items.explanation: Only about 20–25% of HCCs have at least one actionable mutation.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which molecular subclass of HCC is associated with poor outcomes and overexpression of alpha-fetoprotein?
items.explanation: The proliferative subclass is associated with poor outcomes, HBV-related etiologies, and overexpression of alpha-fetoprotein.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which of the following is recommended for primary prevention of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)?
options: Vaccination against HBV and effective treatment of HBV and HCV infection.
Routine use of aspirin, statins, and metformin in high-risk populations.
Annual computed tomography (CT) scans for all individuals over 40 years old.
Surveillance with serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) levels every year.
items.explanation: Vaccination against HBV and effective treatment of HBV and HCV infection are recommended for primary prevention of HCC.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended surveillance method for early detection of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in high-risk patients?
options: Ultrasonography every 6 months with serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) levels.
Biopsy of liver tissue every 6 months.
items.explanation: Ultrasonography every 6 months with serum AFP levels is the recommended surveillance method for early detection of HCC.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.question: Which population is specifically recommended to receive HBV vaccination according to WHO guidelines?
items.explanation: HBV vaccination is recommended for all newborns and high-risk groups, following WHO guidelines.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.question: Why are CT and MRI not recommended as routine screening tools for HCC?
options: They have low sensitivity and specificity.
They lack data on accuracy, are high cost, and may cause harm.
They are less effective than ultrasonography in all patients.
They require invasive procedures to obtain results.
items.explanation: CT and MRI are not recommended as routine screening tools due to lack of data on accuracy, high cost, and possible harm (e.g., radiation with CT).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique is recommended for nodules >1 cm or with AFP ≥20 ng/mL?
items.explanation: Multiphasic contrast-enhanced CT or MRI is recommended for nodules >1 cm or with AFP ≥20 ng/mL.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.question: What is the diagnostic specificity of the typical radiological pattern of HCC using noninvasive techniques?
items.explanation: The diagnostic specificity of the typical radiological pattern of HCC is ~95-100%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.question: What follow-up is recommended for nodules <1 cm in size?
items.explanation: Ultrasound follow-up at 3–4 months is recommended for nodules <1 cm in size.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.question: Which combination improves recurrence-free survival in patients at high risk of recurrence undergoing resection or ablation for early-stage HCC?
items.explanation: Adjuvant therapy with atezolizumab plus bevacizumab improves recurrence-free survival in high-risk patients.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is the median overall survival time for early-stage HCC treated with resection, liver transplantation, or local ablation?
items.explanation: Early-stage treatments like resection and ablation result in median OS times beyond 5 years.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which treatment has improved the natural history of intermediate-stage HCC from 16 months to approximately 30 months?
items.explanation: TACE has improved the natural history of intermediate-stage HCC from 16 months to ~30 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which staging system is most accepted for allocating therapies in HCC and endorsed by U.S. and European guidelines?
items.explanation: The BCLC Classification is the most accepted staging system for HCC.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which combination is recommended as first-line therapy for advanced HCC?
options: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab or durvalumab plus tremelimumab
items.explanation: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab or durvalumab plus tremelimumab are recommended as first-line therapy for advanced HCC.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.question: What is the estimated survival benefit of TACE in patients at intermediate stage with preserved liver function?
items.explanation: Patients at intermediate stage (stage B) benefit from TACE and achieve an estimated survival of 25–30 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which therapies have shown to improve survival compared to placebo in patients with HCC progressing on sorafenib?
items.explanation: Regorafenib, cabozantinib, and ramucirumab have shown to improve survival compared to placebo in patients with HCC progressing on sorafenib.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following criteria are major contraindications for liver resection in patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)?
options: Tumor size <2 cm, Child-Pugh A status
Macrovascular invasion, extrahepatic involvement, and liver dysfunction (Child-Pugh B-C)
Single tumor >5 cm without portal hypertension
Normal bilirubin levels with absence of esophageal varices
items.explanation: Major contraindications for resection include macrovascular invasion, extrahepatic involvement, and liver dysfunction (Child-Pugh B-C).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.question: What is the recommended adjuvant treatment regimen after resection or local ablation in patients at high risk of recurrence for HCC?
items.explanation: An adjuvant regimen with atezolizumab plus bevacizumab for 12 months has been shown to improve recurrence-free survival.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.question: According to the Milan criteria, which patients are considered ideal candidates for liver transplantation in HCC?
options: Cirrhotic patients with single tumors ≤5 cm and portal hypertension
Noncirrhotic patients with multiple nodules each >3 cm
Patients with Child-Pugh C status regardless of tumor size
items.explanation: The Milan criteria include cirrhotic patients with single tumors ≤5 cm and portal hypertension, or small multinodular tumors (three or fewer nodules, each ≤3 cm).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.question: What is the primary ablative technique recommended for thermal ablation in HCC?
items.explanation: The primary ablative technique recommended for thermal ablation is radiofrequency (RF) or microwave (MWA).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.question: What is the typical temperature range for tissue heating during RF ablation?
items.explanation: RF ablation induces coagulative necrosis by heating tissue at 80°–100°C.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: What is the approximate 5-year survival rate for HCC patients treated with RF ablation?
items.explanation: HCC patients treated by RF ablation have a 5-year survival rate of ~60%.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: For which BCLC stage is chemoembolization (TACE) considered the first-line indication?
items.explanation: Chemoembolization TACE is the first-line treatment for patients with intermediate BCLC B stage.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: What percentage of patients experience major complications from chemoembolization (TACE)?
items.explanation: Less than 5% of patients have major complications from TACE.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.question: Which combination therapy has become the standard of care in first-line treatment for advanced-stage HCC?
items.explanation: Atezolizumab plus bevacizumab demonstrated survival differences compared to sorafenib and has become the standard of care.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.question: What is a recommended treatment for patients with varices before starting first-line therapy in advanced HCC?
items.explanation: Screening for varices with upper gastrointestinal endoscopies and the use of one session of banding or carvedilol is recommended.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy showed a median overall survival increase from 7.9 months to 10.7 months in a phase 3 study?
items.explanation: A phase 3 study comparing sorafenib with placebo showed increased survival from 7.9 months to 10.7 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy is indicated for patients with advanced HCC and contraindications for immunotherapies?
items.explanation: Sorafenib or lenvatinib is indicated for patients with contraindications to immunotherapies.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.question: Which systemic therapy for cholangiocarcinoma is associated with FGFR2 fusions and IDH1/2 mutations?
items.explanation: FGFR2 fusions and IDH1/2 mutations are mostly associated with intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.question: Which systemic therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is biomarker-guided based on AFP levels?
items.explanation: Ramucirumab is the only biomarker-guided therapy in HCC based on AFP levels.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.question: What was the median survival for patients receiving ramucirumab compared to placebo in the REACH-2 study?
items.explanation: The median survival for patients receiving ramucirumab was 8.1 months compared to 5 months for those on placebo.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.question: Which parasitic infestation is a prevalent etiology for cholangiocarcinoma (CCA) in Asia?
items.explanation: Opisthorchis viverrini and Clonorchis sinensis are the most common flukes associated with CCA in Asia.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.question: What is a recommended surveillance method for patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) to monitor for iCCA development?
options: Annual imaging techniques and CA 19-9 serum determination
items.explanation: Surveillance in PSC patients is recommended with annual imaging techniques and CA 19-9 serum determination.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.question: Which molecular mutation is associated with the large duct type of iCCA and has a better prognosis?
items.explanation: The large duct type of iCCA is associated with IDH mutations, which are linked to a better prognosis.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.question: Which imaging modality is recommended every 12 months for surveillance in patients with primary biliary sclerosis (PBS) at risk of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma?
items.explanation: Surveillance for patients with PBS includes CA 19-9 and MRCP every 12 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.question: What is the sensitivity and specificity of CA 19-9 at a cutoff level of 100 U/mL for early diagnosis of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma?
items.explanation: CA 19-9 has a sensitivity and specificity of ~60% for early diagnosis.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.question: Which immunohistochemical markers are useful to confirm intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) and distinguish it from metastatic liver cancer?
items.explanation: Immunohistochemistry using K7, K19, and K20 is useful to confirm iCCA.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.question: According to the European Association for the Study of Liver Disease–International Liver Cancer Association (ILCA) guidelines, what is recommended as adjuvant therapy following resection in CCA patients?
options: Adjuvant capecitabine for a period of 6 months
items.explanation: American Society of Clinical Oncology guidelines recommend adjuvant capecitabine for a period of 6 months based on the BILCAP trial.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.question: What is the median reported survival for single tumors in resectable iCCA cases?
items.explanation: The median reported survival for single tumors in resectable iCCA is ~40–50 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.question: What therapy combination showed significantly better survival and progression-free survival compared with gemcitabine plus cisplatin alone in the TOPAZ1 phase 3 trial?
items.explanation: The combination of gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab showed significantly better survival and progression-free survival compared with gemcitabine plus cisplatin alone in the TOPAZ1 trial.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.question: Which therapy has been approved for iCCA patients with FGFR2 fusions based on a phase 2 study?
items.explanation: Pemigatinib, an FGFR2 inhibitor, was approved for iCCA patients with FGFR2 fusions based on a phase 2 study.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.question: Which imaging modalities have good sensitivity and specificity (>85%) for detecting the degree of bile duct involvement in extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma?
items.explanation: CT and MRI are noted for their good sensitivity and specificity in detecting bile duct involvement.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.question: What is the recommended second-step diagnostic procedure after initial imaging assessment for extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma?
options: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiography with brushing and FISH
items.explanation: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiography with brushing to explore cytology and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) is recommended as a second step.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.question: Which of the following is NOT considered a predictor of survival at pathological examination for extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma?
items.explanation: Serum IgG4 levels are used to rule out IgG4 cholangiopathy, not as a predictor of survival.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.question: What is the major risk factor for gallbladder cancer?
items.explanation: Cholelithiasis is identified as the major risk factor for gallbladder cancer.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.question: What percentage of patients with cholelithiasis develop gallbladder cancer?
items.explanation: Less than 1% of patients with cholelithiasis develop gallbladder cancer.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.question: Which imaging technique is most accurate for staging gallbladder cancer?
items.explanation: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography is the most accurate technique for staging gallbladder cancer.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.question: What is the standard of care chemotherapy regimen for advanced gallbladder cancer based on the TOPAZ 1 trial?
items.explanation: The standard of care for advanced gallbladder cancer includes gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.question: Which imaging technique has the highest diagnostic accuracy for focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH) when typical features are present?
items.explanation: MRI has the highest diagnostic accuracy with a specificity of 100% for FNH when typical imaging features are present.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.question: What is the recommended management approach for hepatic adenomas (HCA) smaller than 5 cm?
options: Discontinuation of oral contraceptives and weight loss
items.explanation: For HCA <5 cm, a 1-year follow-up is recommended.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.question: Which benign liver tumor has the highest prevalence in the general population?
items.explanation: Hemangiomas are present in ~5% of the general population, making them the most common benign liver tumor.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.statement: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is more common in cirrhosis associated with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis than in HBV or HCV infection.
items.explanation: HCC is less common in cirrhosis associated with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis compared to HBV or HCV infection.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.statement: Aflatoxin B1 is a fungal carcinogen that induces TP53 mutations.
items.explanation: Aflatoxin B1, present in food supplies, induces TP53 mutations and contributes to HCC development.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.statement: EGFR mutations are common in HCC.
items.explanation: Genes like EGFR, HER2, PIK3CA, BRAF, or KRAS are rarely mutated in HCC (<5%).
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: HBV integration into the genome can occur at driver genes such as TERT promoter.
items.explanation: HBV integrates into the genome of driver genes, including the TERT promoter.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · MOLECULAR PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Aspirin, statins, and metformin are currently recommended as formal chemopreventive strategies for HCC.
items.explanation: Aspirin, statins, and metformin have shown preventive effects but are not yet recommended as formal chemopreventive strategies.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.statement: Surveillance programs for HCC are estimated to engage over 70% of the target population in Europe.
items.explanation: The global implementation of surveillance programs engages ~50% of the target population in Europe.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.statement: A positive biopsy eliminates the diagnosis of HCC.
items.explanation: A negative biopsy does not eliminate the diagnosis of HCC, so a positive result would not necessarily confirm it either.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.statement: AFP levels ≥400 ng/dL are diagnostic of HCC.
items.explanation: AFP levels ≥400 ng/dL are highly suspicious but not diagnostic of HCC.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 2)

items.statement: The Child-Pugh score integrates albumin-bilirubin and MELD scores into its classification.
items.explanation: Child-Pugh does not integrate MELD or albumin-bilirubin scores.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.statement: ECOG performance status is used to determine the stage of HCC in the BCLC system.
items.explanation: ECOG performance status helps define advanced stages in the BCLC staging.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.statement: The TNM staging system is used for HCC because it incorporates liver function and performance status.
items.explanation: The TNM staging system is not used in HCC since it does not incorporate the main prognostic variables related to liver function and performance status.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with end-stage disease should be considered for nutritional, psychological support, and pain management.
items.explanation: Patients with end-stage disease (BCLC D) should receive nutritional and psychological support and proper management of pain.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 2)

items.statement: Liver transplantation cures both the tumor and underlying cirrhosis in HCC patients.
items.explanation: Transplantation simultaneously addresses the tumor and the underlying cirrhosis, offering a cure for both.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.statement: Immunosuppressive regimens after liver transplantation have been proven to prevent recurrence of HCC.
items.explanation: No immunosuppressive regimens or antitumor therapies post-transplantation have demonstrated a preventive effect on recurrence.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.statement: TARE can be safely used in patients with portal vein thrombosis.
items.explanation: Due to the minimally embolic effect of Y-90 microspheres, TARE can be used in patients with portal vein thrombosis.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.statement: DEB-TACE has significantly better clinical outcomes compared to c-TACE.
items.explanation: While DEB-TACE is associated with fewer systemic toxic effects, there are no clear differences in clinical outcomes compared to c-TACE.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.statement: Precision oncology regimens are currently available in HCC due to the high prevalence of actionable molecular aberrations.
items.explanation: Precision oncology regimens are not yet available in HCC because of the low prevalence of actionable molecular aberrations.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.statement: The STRIDE regimen can be administered even in patients with portal hypertension or varices.
items.explanation: Tremelimumab plus durvalumab (STRIDE) can indeed be used in patients with portal hypertension or varices.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.statement: Cabozantinib improves survival in patients progressing to sorafenib with a median of 10.2 months.
items.explanation: Cabozantinib improved survival compared to placebo after progression to sorafenib, with a median of 10.2 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.statement: Regorafenib has been shown to increase survival from 7.8 to 5 months.
items.explanation: Regorafenib increased survival from 7.8 to 10.6 months, not 5 months.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 2) | CHOLANGIO CARCINOMA

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.statement: Sweetened beverages have been reported as a risk factor for the development of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA).
items.explanation: Sweetened beverages are reported as a risk factor for extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (eCCA) and gallbladder carcinoma, not iCCA.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable
items.statement: The most common mutations in perihilar cholangiocarcinoma (pCCA) include P53 (~30%) and KRAS (~25%).
items.explanation: P53 (~30%) and KRAS (~25%) are indeed the most common mutations in pCCA/dCCA.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EPIDEMIOLOGY, RISK FACTORS, AND MOLECULAR TRAITS | Molecular Classification and Actionable

items.statement: In most cases, intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) is diagnosed at an early stage due to routine surveillance.
items.explanation: iCCA is usually diagnosed at advanced stages when symptoms are present.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.statement: PET scanning can help identify lymph node metastasis in patients with no apparent lymph node invasion on MRI and/or CT scan.
items.explanation: Meta-analysis has defined a role for PET scanning in identifying lymph node metastasis.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.statement: External-beam radiation therapy is recommended as standard therapy for CCA.
items.explanation: External-beam radiation therapy is not recommended as standard therapy for CCA.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.statement: Triplet therapy with gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab is considered the standard for managing advanced-stage CCA.
items.explanation: Triplet therapy with gemcitabine, cisplatin, and durvalumab showed significantly better survival and progression-free survival in advanced stages.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.statement: Perihilar cholangiocarcinoma (pCCA) tumors arise from the insertion of the cystic duct to the ampulla of Vater.
items.explanation: pCCA arises between the second-order bile ducts up to the insertion of the cystic duct, not beyond it.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.statement: The BILCAP study supports adjuvant therapy with capecitabine for 6 months.
items.explanation: Adjuvant therapy with capecitabine for 6 months is accepted based on the BILCAP study.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · EXTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.statement: Radiotherapy is effective for gallbladder cancer.
items.explanation: Radiotherapy is not effective for gallbladder cancer.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.statement: Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma typically affects patients with background liver disease.
items.explanation: Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma typically affects children and young adults without background liver disease.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.statement: Follow-up is recommended for hemangiomas.
items.explanation: Conservative management is appropriate for hemangiomas, and follow-up is not recommended.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.statement: Hepatic adenomas are frequently diagnosed in women aged 35–40 years.
items.explanation: HCAs have a low prevalence and are frequently diagnosed in women aged 35–40 years.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · BENIGN LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.assertion: The incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is increasing globally.
items.reason: This increase is due to rising rates of hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection worldwide.
items.explanation: While the burden of liver cancer is indeed increasing, it's not solely attributed to HBV; other factors like HCV and metabolic syndrome also contribute.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.subtopic: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS
items.assertion: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is less common in patients with cirrhosis due to alcohol abuse compared to those with HBV or HCV infection.
items.reason: The annual risk of HCC development in HBV- or HCV-infected cirrhotic patients is 3–8%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HCC is less common in alcohol-related cirrhosis.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND RISK FACTORS

items.assertion: Surveillance for HCC is recommended for patients with advanced cirrhosis.
items.reason: The presence of advanced cirrhosis prevents potentially curative therapies from being employed, thus surveillance is not recommended.
items.explanation: Surveillance is generally not recommended for patients with advanced cirrhosis (Child-Pugh class C) because it prevents potentially curative therapies.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.assertion: A surveillance program yields detection of early HCC in 70–80% of cases.
items.reason: Early-stage tumors are asymptomatic and can be detected through noninvasive radiological methods.
items.explanation: Surveillance programs detect early HCC in 70-80% of cases, as these tumors are often asymptomatic at early stages.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · ☑ PREVENTION AND EARLY DETECTION (part 1)

items.assertion: The BCLC system allows for treatment stage migration.
items.reason: If patients are not candidates for selected therapy, more advanced therapies can be used.
items.explanation: Treatment stage migration is a feature of the BCLC staging system.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: The Child-Pugh class A reflects severe liver dysfunction.
items.reason: Child-Pugh classification assesses liver function with total bilirubin, serum albumin, and other measures.
items.explanation: Child-Pugh class A indicates well-preserved liver function.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: Liver transplantation is limited to less than 5% of HCC cases worldwide.
items.reason: The major limitations include cost and donor availability, which restrict the procedure's accessibility.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why liver transplantation is limited.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.assertion: Living donor liver transplantation should be restricted to centers of excellence.
items.reason: The complexity of the procedure requires specialized expertise in hepatobiliary surgery and transplantation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why it must be restricted to certain centers.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SURGICAL THERAPIES

items.assertion: TARE has been endorsed by clinical practice guidelines.
items.reason: Several phase II studies reported objective responses and overall outcomes with a safe profile similar to TACE.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the endorsement of TARE.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.assertion: TACE can be applied to all patients at intermediate stage.
items.reason: TACE requires no specific contraindications related to liver function or tumor burden.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; TACE can only be applied to about 50% of patients due to contraindications like liver failure and massive tumor burden.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES

items.assertion: Sorafenib is associated with a median treatment duration of about 6 months.
items.reason: Treatment discontinuation due to toxicity, advanced age, or liver failure occurs in approximately one-third of patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the median treatment duration.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.assertion: Lenvatinib is noninferior to sorafenib in terms of overall survival.
items.reason: A phase 3 study showed lenvatinib had a median OS of 13.6 months compared to 12.3 months for sorafenib (HR, 0.92).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why lenvatinib is noninferior.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · SYSTEMIC THERAPIES (part 1)

items.assertion: CA 19-9 is used as a tumor biomarker with prognostic significance for intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma.
items.reason: CA 19-9 lacks accuracy (sensitivity and specificity of ~60%) for early diagnosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the prognostic significance.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · INTRAHEPATIC CHOLANGIOCARCINOMA

items.assertion: Patients with multinodular unresectable tumors can be considered for locoregional therapies.
items.reason: Locoregional therapies, such as chemoembolization or radioembolization, are recommended based on cohort studies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with multinodular unresectable tumors might be considered for locoregional therapies.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.assertion: Pembrolizumab is approved for MSI-H or dMMR solid tumors that progressed following prior treatment.
items.reason: This approval was based on a basket tissue-agnostic trial excluding patients receiving durvalumab in the first line.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the basis for pembrolizumab's approval.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · TREATMENT

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.assertion: Surgical resection is the mainstay of treatment for fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma.
items.reason: Fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma has a better prognosis than HCC due to earlier age of presentation and absence of cirrhosis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why surgical resection is the mainstay treatment.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS

items.subtopic: GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
items.assertion: Low AFP <100 ng/mL indicates a good prognosis in hepatoblastoma.
items.reason: AFP levels are used as a prognostic marker in hepatoblastoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; low AFP <100 ng/mL actually indicates poor prognosis in hepatoblastoma.
items.reference: Tumors of the Liver and Biliary Tree · GALLBLADDER CANCER | OTHER MALIGNANT LIVER TUMORS
